title: emperor
description: First emperor of unified China. Unified Warring States 221 BCE. Standardized script, weights, currency. Initiated the Great Wall and Terracotta Army. Buried at Mausoleum of the First Qin Emperor (UNESCO 441).
ethical_notes: ETHICS-007: Burning of books, burying of scholars (213-212 BCE) — systematic intellectual purge targeting Confucian texts and dissent. Corvée deaths for Great Wall construction estimated in hundreds of thousands. Mass-scale forced labor documented in Shiji and Han critiques.

sources.citation: Sima Qian (~100 BCE). Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian).
Bodde, D. (1986). Cambridge History of China, Vol. 1: The Ch'in and Han.

title: empress regnant
description: Only empress regnant in Chinese imperial history. Founded short-lived Zhou dynasty (690-705) usurping Tang. Buddhist patron. Reforms including merit-based imperial examinations expansion.
ethical_notes: Traditional Confucian historiography (Zizhi Tongjian 11th c.) demonized her as cruel usurper — likely anti-female bias. Modern revisionist scholarship (Guisso 1978, Woo 2008) offers balanced reassessment. Forced abdication in 705 by coup restored Tang.

sources.citation: Twitchett, D. (1979). Cambridge History of China, Vol. 3.
Rothschild, N. H. (2008). Wu Zhao: China's Only Woman Emperor. Pearson.

title: khagan / emperor
description: Founder of Yuan Dynasty (1271). Grandson of Genghis Khan. Conquered Southern Song 1279, unifying China for first time under Mongol rule. Received Marco Polo at Dadu (Khanbaliq, modern Beijing).
ethical_notes: Two failed invasions of Japan (1274, 1281) — second destroyed by kamikaze typhoon. Invasions of Vietnam (Đại Việt), Java (Singhasari) also failed. Yuan-era Chinese population classified in 4-tier ethnic hierarchy (Mongol / Semu / Han / Nanren), institutionalized discrimination.

sources.citation: Rossabi, M. (1988). Khubilai Khan: His Life and Times. California.

title: dictator
description: Roman general and statesman. Gallic Wars (58-50 BCE) conquered modern France. Crossed the Rubicon 49 BCE triggering civil war. Victor over Pompey. Dictator perpetuo 44 BCE. Assassinated Ides of March.
ethical_notes: Gallic Wars: estimated 1 million dead + 1 million enslaved (Caesar's own Commentarii). At Uxellodunum (51 BCE) ordered hands severed of all survivors. Ethnic cleansing of Eburones (Caesar De Bello Gallico 8.24). Described by Plutarch and Cassius Dio as exceptionally brutal even by Roman standards.

sources.citation: Caesar (c. 50 BCE). Commentarii de Bello Gallico.
Goldsworthy, A. (2006). Caesar: Life of a Colossus. Yale.

title: emperor
description: First Roman emperor. Julius Caesar's adopted heir. Victory at Actium 31 BCE ended Republic. Established principate. 41-year reign, Pax Romana begins. Died at Nola; institutionally deified.

sources.citation: Syme, R. (1939). The Roman Revolution. Oxford.

title: emperor
description: Roman emperor. Victory at Milvian Bridge 312 (Chi-Rho vision). Edict of Milan 313 legalized Christianity. Founded Constantinople 330. Council of Nicaea 325 established Trinitarian orthodoxy.
ethical_notes: Execution of eldest son Crispus 326 + wife Fausta 326 — circumstances disputed in sources. Pagan temple confiscations post-326 initiated gradual Christian hegemony. Nicaea proceedings coerced orthodoxy through imperial power (Gibbon critique).

sources.citation: Barnes, T. D. (1981). Constantine and Eusebius. Harvard.

title: emperor
description: King of Franks 768-814, King of Lombards 774, Holy Roman Emperor from 25 Dec 800 (coronation by Leo III). Unified Western Europe. Carolingian Renaissance.
ethical_notes: ETHICS-007: Massacre of Verden 782 — ~4,500 Saxon captives executed after refusal to convert. Forced Christianization of Saxons continued to 804. Avar campaigns 791-803 caused demographic collapse in Pannonia. Medieval hagiography sanitized these.

sources.citation: Einhard (~830). Vita Karoli Magni.
McKitterick, R. (2008). Charlemagne: The Formation of a European Identity. Cambridge.

title: khagan
description: Founder of Mongol Empire, proclaimed 'Universal Ruler' at 1206 kurultai. Unified Turco-Mongol tribes. Conquered Xi Xia, Jin, Khwarezmian. Largest contiguous land empire in history at peak.
ethical_notes: ETHICS-007: Khwarezmian campaign 1219-1221 genocidal scale — Herat (1.5-2M est. dead), Balkh total razing, Samarkand + Bukhara sackings. Nishapur massacre 1221 (wife beheaded retaliation). Total campaign deaths 5-30M (modern estimates; contemporary sources 40M but likely inflated). Demographic collapse in Iran/Central Asia.

sources.citation: Secret History of the Mongols (c. 1240).
May, T. (2012). The Mongol Conquests in World History. Reaktion.

title: emir
description: Central Asian Turco-Mongol conqueror. Founder of Timurid Empire. Sacked Delhi 1398, Baghdad 1401. Died while preparing invasion of Ming China. Buried at Gur-e-Amir, Samarkand.
ethical_notes: ETHICS-007: Delhi sack 1398 estimated 100,000 civilian deaths; pyramids of skulls at Isfahan 1387 (~70,000), Baghdad 1401 (~90,000). Described by Ibn Arabshah (hostile) and Sharafuddin Yazdi (favorable) — both confirm industrial-scale massacres. Total campaign estimate 17M deaths (5% world population then).

sources.citation: Sharafuddin Yazdi (~1430). Zafarnama.
Manz, B. F. (1989). The Rise and Rule of Tamerlane. Cambridge.

title: emperor
description: Third Mauryan emperor. Kalinga War 262-261 BCE conversion experience. Rock and Pillar Edicts (~33 inscriptions in Brahmi/Kharoshthi/Aramaic/Greek) promoting Dhamma, non-violence. Buddhist missions reached Sri Lanka, Hellenistic Mediterranean.
ethical_notes: Pre-Kalinga campaigns conventional Mauryan expansionism. Kalinga War edict (Rock Edict 13) states 100,000 killed, 150,000 deported — among first imperial self-critiques in history. Ashoka's Dhamma non-violent reforms contested as rhetoric vs. reality by some modern historians (Thapar).

sources.citation: Thapar, R. (1961). Ashoka and the Decline of the Mauryas. Oxford.

title: emperor
description: Third Mughal emperor. Abolished jizya 1564. Religious dialogue (Din-i Ilahi, Ibadat Khana). Central imperial administration (Ain-i Akbari). Victory at Panipat II 1556. Marriage alliances with Rajput kingdoms. Fatehpur Sikri capital.
ethical_notes: Chittor siege 1568 — ~30,000 civilian massacre post-surrender. Forced conversions minority. Syncretic policies tolerated by orthodoxy but criticized posthumously by Aurangzeb's faction.

sources.citation: Richards, J. F. (1995). The Mughal Empire. Cambridge.

title: emperor
description: Sixth Mughal emperor. Territorial peak (Deccan expansion). Restored Sharia orthodoxy. War of succession vs. Dara Shikoh (executed 1659). 26 years continuous Deccan wars (1681-1707). Empire bankrupt and overstretched at death.
ethical_notes: ETHICS-007: Jizya tax restoration 1679. Hindu temple demolitions documented (Kashi Vishwanath Varanasi 1669, Keshavadeva Mathura 1670 — though scope contested scholarly between Truschke vs. Eaton). Execution of Sikh Guru Teg Bahadur 1675 for refusing conversion. Dara Shikoh execution for alleged Hindu sympathies.

sources.citation: Eaton, R. M. (2000). Temple Desecration and Indo-Muslim States. Journal of Islamic Studies.
Truschke, A. (2017). Aurangzeb: The Life and Legacy of India's Most Controversial King. Penguin.

title: mansa
description: Tenth mansa of Mali Empire. 1324 hajj to Mecca via Cairo — destabilized Egyptian gold prices for 12 years (al-Umari). Built Djinguereber Mosque Timbuktu (1327). Possibly richest person in history (inflation-adjusted estimates).
ethical_notes: Mali Empire participation in trans-Saharan slave trade — documented by al-Bakri (11th c.), Ibn Battuta (1352 visit). Royal court included enslaved Tuareg and Soninke.

sources.citation: al-Umari (1342). Masalik al-absar.
Levtzion, N. (1973). Ancient Ghana and Mali. Methuen.

title: sultan
description: Kurdish founder of Ayyubid Sultanate. Ended Fatimid Caliphate 1171. Victory at Hattin 4 July 1187 retook Jerusalem from Crusaders (88 years of Frankish rule ended). Third Crusade stalemate with Richard I 1191-1192. Died in Damascus.
ethical_notes: Templar/Hospitaller prisoners executed at Hattin 1187. However, Jerusalem surrender terms allowed Christian ransom (80 dinars male, 40 female, 20 child) rather than massacre — contrast to 1099 Crusader Jerusalem conquest. Western medieval chivalric reputation built on this moderation.

sources.citation: Lyons, M. C. & Jackson, D. E. P. (1982). Saladin: The Politics of the Holy War. Cambridge.

title: sultan
description: Tenth Ottoman sultan. Peak Ottoman expansion: Belgrade 1521, Rhodes 1522, Mohács 1526 (ended medieval Hungarian kingdom), Vienna siege 1529 (failed), Baghdad 1534. Kanuni ('Lawgiver') reforms. Patron of Mimar Sinan architecture.
ethical_notes: Devşirme system (Christian boys forcibly converted to become Janissaries) at peak intensity. Execution of son Mustafa 1553, grandson Bayezid 1561 — Ottoman succession violence. Hurrem Sultan influence contested as harem power-grab vs. legitimate co-rulership.

sources.citation: Imber, C. (2019). The Ottoman Empire, 1300-1650. Bloomsbury.

title: sapa inca
description: Ninth Sapa Inca. Transformed Cuzco kingdom into Tawantinsuyu empire through conquests 1438+. Built Machu Picchu, Sacsayhuamán, Ollantaytambo. Reorganized state with Qullqa storehouses, Qhapaq Ñan road system, Mit'a labor tax.
ethical_notes: Mitmaq (population resettlement) used for political control — families forcibly moved across empire. Chanka 1438 victory followed by mass executions. Yanakuna hereditary servants outside Ayllu kin structures = form of bondage.

sources.citation: D'Altroy, T. N. (2002). The Incas. Blackwell.

title: huey tlatoani
description: Ninth Tlatoani of Mēxihco (Aztec Triple Alliance). Peak empire extent. Received Cortés Nov 1519 at Tenochtitlan. Died 1 July 1520 during Spanish captivity — cause disputed (Spanish sources: stoned by own people; Mexica sources: killed by Spanish).
ethical_notes: Mass human sacrifices at Huey Teocalli documented in Florentine Codex + Spanish conquistador reports (numbers inflated by Spanish to justify conquest; modern scholarly estimates ~20,000/year peak). Cholula massacre 1519 by Cortés + Tlaxcaltec allies ~3,000-6,000 unarmed dead preceded Moctezuma's encounter.

sources.citation: Sahagún (1569). Florentine Codex, Book 12.
Townsend, C. (2019). Fifth Sun: A New History of the Aztecs. Oxford.

title: king
description: King of Macedon. Conquered Persian Empire in 10 years (334-323 BCE). Founded ~20 cities (Alexandria Egypt principal). Died at Babylon age 32. Empire immediately fragmented into Diadochi kingdoms (Ptolemaic, Seleucid, Antigonid).
ethical_notes: Destruction of Thebes 335 BCE (~6,000 killed, 30,000 enslaved). Siege of Tyre 332 BCE (~8,000 killed in final assault, 30,000 enslaved). Gaza massacre 332 BCE. Mass marriages at Susa 324 — coercive cultural integration of Persian elite. Divine self-proclamation at Siwa 331 — deified in life.

sources.citation: Arrian (2nd c. CE). Anabasis Alexandri.
Worthington, I. (2014). By the Spear: Philip II, Alexander the Great. Oxford.
